text: The influence of small additions of Ti, Zr, and Hf on the mechanical properties of a modified Hastelloy N with the nominal composition Ni-12% Mo-7% Cr-0.2% Mn-0.05% C is described in this report.
It deals specifically with test results from numerous, small, laboratory melts and several 100-lb melts from commercial vendors.
Generally, good properties were observed when the irradiation temperature was $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ or less and poor when the temperature was $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ or higher.

Previous studies $^{1-4}$ showed Hastelloy N (Ni-16% Mo-7% Cr-4% Fe-0.05% C) susceptible to a type of damage produced by irradiation at high temperatures that results in reduced stress-rupture properties and fracture ductility.
We initially modified the base composition to Ni-12% Mo-7% Cr-0.2% Mn-0.05% C to obtain an alloy that

We then made a number of laboratory melts with various additions of Ti, Zr, and Hf, since they form very strong, stable diborides<sup>5</sup> and should tie up the boron as compounds rather than allow it to segregate to the grain boundaries.
The more random distribution of the boron should reduce the radiation damage by reducing the concentration at grain boundaries of helium produced by the $^{10}\mathrm{B}(n,\alpha)$ reaction.
A starting charge of about 2 lb was consolidated and melted several times.
The alloy was again melted and drop cast into a l-in.-diam $\times$ 6-in.-long ingot.

The ingot was swaged to $1/4$ -in.-diam rod by the following schedule: from l in. to $3/4$ in. at $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , from $3/4$ in. to $7/16$ in. at $871^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and from $7/16$ in. to $1/4$ in. at ambient temperature.
The commercial melts were melted by vacuum induction and cast into six 4-in.-diam ingots that weighed about 16 lb each.
They were initially forged at $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ; the final working was done at room temperature.
The final product was small 1/2-in.-thick plates with $40\%$ cold work.

A core facility was used in the ETR where the thermal and fast ( $>1\mathrm{MeV}$ ) fluxes were each $3.2 \times 10^{14}$ neutrons $\mathrm{cm}^{-2} \sec^{-1}$ and the fluence was $6 \times 10^{20}$ neutrons/ $\mathrm{cm}^2$ .
The ETR experiments were uninstrumented, and the design temperatures were either below $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ or $600 \pm 100^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

The experiments in the ORR were performed in a poolside facility where the peak fluxes were $6 \times 10^{13}$ neutrons $\mathrm{cm}^{-2} \sec^{-1}$ (thermal) and $5 \times 10^{12}$ neutrons $\mathrm{cm}^{-2} \sec^{-1}$ ( $>2.9$ MeV).
either one or two cycles so that the thermal fluence was 2 to $5 \times 10^{20}$ neutrons/cm $^2$ and the fast fluence was 2 to $4 \times 10^{19}$ neutrons/cm $^2$ .
These experiments were instrumented, and the temperatures were controlled at 650 to $871^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

The experiments in the MSRE were run in the center of the core, where the thermal flux was $4.1 \times 10^{12}$ neutrons cm $^{-2}$ sec $^{-1}$ and the fast ( $>1.22$ MeV) flux was $1.0 \times 10^{12}$ neutrons cm $^{-2}$ sec $^{-1}$ .

The samples were exposed to a noncorrosive fluoride salt environment for 4800 hr at $645 \pm 10^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and received a thermal fluence of $1.3 \times 10^{20}$ neutrons/cm $^2$ and a fast fluence of $3.1 \times 10^{19}$ neutrons/cm $^2$ .
The accuracy of the strain measurements is about $\pm 0.1\%$ , considering the effects of ambient temperature variations in the hot cell, mechanical vibrations, etc.
The systems for measuring and controlling temperature combine to give a temperature uncertainty of about $\pm 1\%$ .
These alloys were of the nominal base composition of Ni-12% Mo-7% Cr-0.2% Mn-0.05% C.
Typical microstructures of these alloys after a l-hr anneal at 1177°C are shown in Fig.
The second group, alloys 75 and 76, contained 1% Ti and were made to explore the influence of varying carbon content.
The third material, a 100-lb commercial alloy from Special Metals Corporation, designated heat 21545, contained 0.49% Ti.

This alloy had a nominal composition quite close to that of alloy 104, and the microstructures of the two alloys after a l-hr anneal at 1177°C were quite similar except that the commercial alloy contained patches of precipitate.
The grain size after annealing for 100 hr at 871°C was quite fine, and copious quantities of carbide precipitates were present.
Alloys 100 through 107 were tested under a variety of conditions.

Tensile tests were run at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ on samples that had been annealed 1 hr at $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and aged 1000 hr at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to duplicate the thermal history of irradiated samples.
The results of these tests are summarized in Table 2.

The tensile strength varied in a poorly defined manner: at a strain rate of $0.05\mathrm{min}^{-1}$ , it increased systematically with increasing titanium, and at a rate of $0.002\mathrm{min}^{-1}$ it reached a maximum at $0.55\%$ Ti.
At a strain rate of $0.05\mathrm{min}^{-1}$ , the addition of $0.11\%$ Ti greatly increased the fracture strain; further additions decreased the fracture strain.
At a strain rate of $0.002\mathrm{min}^{-1}$ , the fracture strain improved greatly with the addition of $0.11\%$ Ti, and further additions caused a gradual improvement.

The most dramatic effect of irradiation temperature is demonstrated by the combination of irradiation at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and testing at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (compare the results in Tables 7 and 10).
For example, alloy 107 was irradiated at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and tested at 32,400 psi and $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The rupture life was 2928.8 hr, the minimum creep rate was $0.0026\% / \mathrm{hr}$ , and the fracture strain was $12.8\%$ .

The same alloy irradiated at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and tested at 30,000 psi and $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ had a rupture life of only 49.4 hr, a minimum creep rate of $0.024\% / \mathrm{hr}$ , and a fracture strain of $1.6\%$ .
Alloys 75 and 76 were similar in composition to alloy 107, and only limited testing has been carried out.
The results of tensile tests are summarized in Table 11.
The results for the unirradiated samples agree well with those for alloy 107 (Table 2).
Alloy 75 contains $0.062\%$ C, and alloy 76 contains $0.117\%$ C; therefore, the trends of higher strength and lower fracture strain for alloy 76 are reasonable.
Irradiation increased strength and decreased fracture strain (Table 11).
The fracture strains agree reasonably well with those for alloy 107 irradiated at $650^{\circ}$ C (Table 3) and $50^{\circ}$ C (Table 4).
The samples annealed for 100 hr at $871^{\circ}$ C had a very small grain size, and the fracture strains were lower for these samples.
The higher carbon content of alloy 76 resulted in slightly lower fracture strains in tensile tests.

As already shown, alloy 21546 exhibited a change in properties after irradiation that correlated well with changes in the carbide structure (Figs.
Further work by Gehlbach and $\mathrm{Cook}^{10}$ showed that Ti, Hf, and Zr promoted the formation of the MC type of carbides.

The normal type of carbide for Hastelloy N is $\mathbf{M}_2\mathbf{C}$ when the silicon content is low and $\mathbf{M}_6\mathbf{C}$ when the silicon content is high.[11] The work of Gehlbach and $\mathrm{Cook}^{10}$ also showed that low levels (about $0.5\%$ ) of Ti, Hf, and Zr can result in an MC type of carbide at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and a relatively coarse $\mathbf{M}_2\mathbf{C}$ type at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Because of the difference in the type of carbide precipitated at 650 and $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in many of these alloys, aging at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ seems to produce larger changes in properties.
The precipitation of copious quantities of $\mathbf{ZrC}$ in alloys that contain less than $0.5\%$ $\mathbf{Zr}$ is quite dramatic, but the changes in properties are minimal.

This first phase of this study of the effects of additions of Ti, Zr, and Hf to a base alloy of Ni-12% Mo-7% Cr-0.2% Mn-0.05% C dealt with small, laboratory melts and the first 100-lb commercial melts.
Tests on samples irradiated at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ or less show that the rupture lives at a given stress level and the fracture strains are improved markedly.
Irradiation at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ results in very poor properties.
